topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee shop buzzed with a cheerful, oblivious energy. I watched Liam laugh, a bright, unguarded sound that used to be the soundtrack to my entire adolescence. He was talking animatedly to a new acquaintance, his hand gesturing wildly, completely unaware of the quiet tremor running through my chest. Every time he turned his head, a sharp, cold feeling settled in my stomach, a heavy weight pressing down on my lungs. I tried to focus on my lukewarm latte, tracing the condensation rings on the table, but the noise seemed to amplify, becoming a relentless, grating static. When he finally glanced my way, his eyes registered me for a fraction of a second—a flicker, then nothing. The casualness of it was a physical blow. I pulled my sweater tighter around myself, the fabric suddenly too thick, and mumbled an apology for taking up space, my voice sounding thin and reedy even to my own ears. I needed to leave, needed the solid ground of solitude, but my feet felt rooted to the floor, unable to move toward the exit.

[story 2]

It was the silence of the old attic that truly suffocated me. Dust motes danced in the single shaft of sunlight piercing the gloom, illuminating boxes filled with faded photographs. I held up a picture—us, grinning maniacally on a beach trip—and the smile felt alien, pasted on someone else’s face. I kept turning the photo over and over, my thumb smudging the edges. A tightness began to constrict my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I remembered every shared secret, every inside joke, every promise whispered under the stars. Yet, when I tried to conjure Liam’s face in my mind, the image remained stubbornly blank, like a page wiped clean. I dropped the photo onto a trunk with a dull thud. My hands started shaking, a fine, uncontrollable vibration that made the surrounding objects seem to wobble. I needed air, desperately, but the air in the small space felt thick and stale, trapping me in a suffocating bubble of disbelief.

[story 3]

The party was a kaleidoscope of loud music and forced camaraderie. I navigated the crowd, nodding at familiar faces, searching for the anchor I desperately needed. Then I saw him. Liam. He was laughing with a group, his arm draped casually around someone else’s shoulder. My heart executed a sudden, violent lurch against my ribs. I approached tentatively, the words catching in my throat like shards of glass. "Hey," I managed, the sound barely audible above the bass thumping through the floorboards. He turned, and for a moment, his expression was neutral, polite. It was the kind of blankness that suggested he hadn't recognized the anomaly standing before him. The warmth I had banked up all evening seemed to drain out instantly, leaving behind a chilling emptiness. I felt a strange detachment, as if I were watching a poorly dubbed film of my own life. I took a step back, my gaze darting around the room, seeking any escape route from the suffocating proximity of his indifference.
